datom 0.0.0.9000

    • Clinical Data Versioning with datom
    • Credential Management
    • Team Collaboration with datom

Team Collaboration with datom

Source: vignettes/team-collaboration.Rmd
team-collaboration.Rmd

Overview

datom supports multiple developers working on the same data repository concurrently. Git tracks metadata; S3 stores data. This separation means developers can work in parallel as long as they follow a simple discipline: pull before you work, push when you’re done.

Prerequisites: This vignette assumes credentials are already configured. See vignette("credentials") for setup instructions including AWS keys, github_pat, and store construction.

This vignette covers:

  • One-time repository setup (who runs datom_init_repo())
  • How teammates join an existing repository
  • The daily pull → receive → sync workflow
  • What to do when sync is rejected (push conflicts)
  • Setting up read-only access for analysts

Roles

datom recognizes two user types:

Role Git access S3 access Typical user
Developer Read + write Read + write Data engineer, receives extracts, runs datom_sync()
Reader None Read only Statistician, analyst, reads versioned tables

Developers have a local clone of the git repo. Readers connect directly to S3 using datom_get_conn() with a store and project name.

One-Time Setup: Initializing the Repository

One person initializes the repository. Everyone else clones it.

library(datom)

# Build a store (see vignette("credentials") for details)
s3 <- datom_store_s3(
  bucket     = "clinical-data-bucket",
  prefix     = "study-001/",
  access_key = keyring::key_get("AWS_ACCESS_KEY"),
  secret_key = keyring::key_get("AWS_SECRET_KEY")
)

store <- datom_store(
  governance = s3,
  data       = s3,
  github_pat = keyring::key_get("GITHUB_PAT")
)

# The lead data engineer runs this once
datom_init_repo(
  path         = "study_001_data",
  project_name = "STUDY_001",
  store        = store,
  create_repo  = TRUE,
  repo_name    = "study-001-data"
)
#> ✔ Created GitHub repo "study-001-data"
#> ✔ Initialized datom repository at study_001_data
#> ✔ Pushed to origin/main
#> ✔ Synced dispatch.json and manifest.json to S3

This creates the folder structure, creates a GitHub repo, pushes the initial commit, and uploads dispatch.json and manifest.json to S3.

S3 Namespace Safety

datom_init_repo() checks whether the S3 namespace is already occupied before writing any files. If another project has already claimed s3://clinical-data-bucket/study-001/datom/.metadata/manifest.json, you’ll get a clear error:

datom_init_repo(
  path         = "my_project",
  project_name = "MY_PROJECT",
  store        = store
)
#> ✖ S3 namespace already occupied by project "STUDY_001".
#>   Location: s3://clinical-data-bucket/study-001/datom/
#> ℹ Use a different bucket or prefix, or pass `.force = TRUE` to override.

This prevents accidental overwrites when multiple teams share an S3 bucket.

Joining an Existing Repository

Every other team member clones with datom_clone(). They need a store with the remote_url of the existing repo:

# Teammate builds their own store with the remote URL
store <- datom_store(
  governance = datom_store_s3(
    bucket     = "clinical-data-bucket",
    prefix     = "study-001/",
    access_key = keyring::key_get("AWS_ACCESS_KEY"),
    secret_key = keyring::key_get("AWS_SECRET_KEY")
  ),
  data = datom_store_s3(
    bucket     = "clinical-data-bucket",
    prefix     = "study-001/",
    access_key = keyring::key_get("AWS_ACCESS_KEY"),
    secret_key = keyring::key_get("AWS_SECRET_KEY")
  ),
  github_pat = keyring::key_get("GITHUB_PAT"),
  remote_url = "https://github.com/org/study-001-data.git"
)

conn <- datom_clone(path = "study_001_data", store = store)
#> ✔ Cloned "STUDY_001" to study_001_data
print(conn)
#> ── datom connection
#> • Project: "STUDY_001"
#> • Role: "developer"
#> • Bucket: "clinical-data-bucket"
#> • Prefix: "study-001/"
#> • Region: "us-east-1"
#> • Path: /home/bob/study_001_data

datom_clone() wraps git2r::clone() and returns a ready-to-use connection — no re-initialization needed.

Daily Workflow

Step 1: Pull Latest Changes

Every work session starts with datom_pull():

conn <- datom_get_conn(path = "study_001_data")

datom_pull(conn)
#> ✔ Pulled 3 commits on "main".

datom_pull() fetches and merges upstream git commits — including any metadata changes from teammates (manifest updates, new table versions, dispatch changes). Git is the source of truth for all metadata.

If you’re already up to date:

#> ℹ Already up to date on "main".

Note: You don’t strictly need to call datom_pull() manually before every datom_sync(). datom’s push mechanism automatically pulls before pushing to ensure you’re up to date. However, calling datom_pull() at the start of a session is still recommended — it lets you see what teammates have changed before you begin your own work.

Step 2: Receive Data and Sync

Place new data extracts in input_files/, then scan and sync. These are intentionally separate steps:

# Example: write a data extract to the input directory
dm <- datom_example_data("dm", cutoff_date = "2026-03-28")
write.csv(dm, "study_001_data/input_files/dm.csv", row.names = FALSE)

# Scan — detects new and changed files in input_files/
manifest <- datom_sync_manifest(conn)

# Inspect before syncing — what did datom find?
manifest
#>   table_name status      file_path
#> 1         dm changed  input_files/dm.csv

The scan step produces a manifest that you can inspect before committing to a sync. This is your checkpoint: verify that the right files were detected and that statuses (new, changed, unchanged) make sense before writing anything to git or S3.

# Sync — imports, converts to parquet, writes to git + S3
results <- datom_sync(conn, manifest)
#> ℹ Syncing 1 table...
#> ✔ dm synced (changed).
#> ℹ Sync complete: 1 succeeded, 0 failed, 0 skipped.

Why two steps? Separating scan from sync gives you a chance to review what will be synced before anything is written. In a team setting this is especially valuable — you can catch stale files, accidental duplicates, or unexpected changes before they hit git and S3. Future versions of datom will expand manifest-level APIs for richer pre-sync inspection (e.g., adding table descriptions, tagging QA status).

Step 3: Verify 

# Check repository consistency
datom_validate(conn)
#> ✔ All checks passed. Git and S3 are consistent.

datom_validate() confirms that your local git state and S3 agree — metadata matches data, all referenced parquet files exist in S3, and nothing is missing or orphaned. Run it after every sync session.

That’s it. The sync committed to git, pushed, and uploaded data + metadata to S3. Your teammates can now datom_pull() to see the new version.

Handling Conflicts

Scenario: Push Rejected

If a teammate pushed while you were syncing, datom_sync() will abort with a clear message:

#> ✖ Local git branch "main" is behind remote by 2 commits.
#> ℹ Run `datom_pull()` or `git pull` to update before syncing.

Resolution:

# 1. Pull the latest changes
datom_pull(conn)

# 2. Re-scan (manifest may have changed)
manifest <- datom_sync_manifest(conn)

# 3. Re-sync
results <- datom_sync(conn, manifest)

Scenario: Merge Conflict

Merge conflicts occur when two developers modify the same file (e.g., both update metadata.json for the same table). This is rare in practice because datom auto-generates metadata files — but it can happen if developers sync the same table simultaneously.

#> ✖ Merge conflict detected — manual resolution required.
#> ℹ Pull latest changes, resolve conflicts, and re-run.
#> ℹ Use `git status` to see conflicting files.

Resolution:

  1. Open a terminal in the repo directory
  2. Run git status to see conflicting files
  3. Edit the files to resolve conflicts (or accept one side with git checkout --theirs)
  4. git add the resolved files and git commit
  5. Re-run datom_sync()

Tip: Merge conflicts are minimized when each developer is responsible for different tables. Coordinate who syncs which tables within the team.

Setting Up Readers

Readers (analysts, statisticians) don’t need git access. They connect directly to S3 using a reader store:

# Reader store — no github_pat
reader_s3 <- datom_store_s3(
  bucket     = "clinical-data-bucket",
  prefix     = "study-001/",
  access_key = keyring::key_get("AWS_ACCESS_KEY_READER"),
  secret_key = keyring::key_get("AWS_SECRET_KEY_READER")
)

reader_store <- datom_store(governance = reader_s3, data = reader_s3)

reader_conn <- datom_get_conn(store = reader_store, project_name = "STUDY_001")
print(reader_conn)
#> ── datom connection
#> • Project: "STUDY_001"
#> • Role: "reader"
#> • Bucket: "clinical-data-bucket"
#> • Prefix: "study-001/"
#> • Region: "us-east-1"

Readers can list tables, read data, and view history — all via S3:

datom_list(reader_conn)
datom_read(reader_conn, "dm")
datom_history(reader_conn, "dm")

What to Share with Readers

Provide your readers with:

Item Example
project_name "STUDY_001"
bucket "clinical-data-bucket"
prefix "study-001/"
AWS credentials Reader-scoped IAM keys (read-only S3 access)

For credential setup details, see vignette("credentials").

Recommended Team Practices

1. One person initializes, everyone else clones 

# Alice (lead):
datom_init_repo(
  path = "study_data", project_name = "STUDY_001",
  store = store, create_repo = TRUE, repo_name = "study-001-data"
)

# Bob and Carol (build store with remote_url, then clone):
conn <- datom_clone(path = "study_data", store = store)

2. Pull at the start of every session 

# First line of every script
conn <- datom_get_conn(path = "study_data")
datom_pull(conn)

3. Divide tables across developers

If Alice syncs dm and lb, and Bob syncs ex and vs, merge conflicts are virtually impossible.

4. Validate after sync 

datom_validate(conn)

This confirms git and S3 are consistent. Run it after every sync session, and especially after resolving conflicts.

5. Use datom_status() to see what’s changed 

datom_status(conn)

Before syncing, check which tables have pending changes. This helps avoid syncing stale data or re-syncing unchanged tables.

Summary

Step Command Who
Build store datom_store(...) Everyone (once per session)
Initialize (once) datom_init_repo(store = ...) Lead developer
Clone datom_clone(store = ...) All other developers
Start session datom_pull(conn) All developers
Receive data Place files in input_files/ Developer
Scan datom_sync_manifest(conn) Developer
Sync datom_sync(conn, manifest) Developer
Validate datom_validate(conn) Developer
Read data datom_read(conn, "table") Developer or reader
Conflict? datom_pull() → re-scan → re-sync Developer
